skill.name: skill-name
skill.description: Security operations skill template for building AppSec, DevSecOps, threat modeling, compliance, and incident response workflows. Use when: creating structured security review workflows, drafting security rule templates, building CI security scan examples, or organizing remediation guidance with OWASP, CWE, MITRE ATT&CK, and compliance mappings.
skill.summary: Create structured security operations workflows, rule templates, and CI security scan guidance.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, secret, filesystem, and script patterns. Most are false positives from security training examples and rule templates, but the CI asset contains a real remote installer piped directly into a shell. No confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection was found, but the unsafe CI pattern and unfinished template quality make this unsuitable for publication without remediation.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
high_findings.description: The CI template downloads a third-party installer over the network and executes it directly with a shell. This is a true positive because users copying the template would run unauthenticated remote code in CI unless they replace this pattern with a pinned, verified installation method.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static pattern is confirmed by semantic context: a CI pipeline step performs network download and immediate shell execution. It is presented as a reusable template, so the supply-chain risk is real even without malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Executable CI Template Requires Trust Review
medium_findings.description: The CI asset is designed to run security scanners, parse reports, read generated files, and post pull request comments. These actions are legitimate for DevSecOps, but they should be reviewed before publication because they combine external commands, filesystem reads, network-based actions, and GitHub token access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The operations match the stated CI security scanning purpose, but the combined permissions and execution behavior require maintainer review. I did not find evidence that secrets are exfiltrated.

medium_findings.title: Unfinished Generic Skill Template
medium_findings.description: The primary skill file still contains placeholders for name, maintainer, category, patterns, troubleshooting, and examples. This is not a direct exploit, but it increases operational risk because users may copy incomplete security guidance into production workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The placeholder content is directly visible in the skill metadata and body. The risk is content quality and misuse, not hidden malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Security Example Snippets Trigger Static Patterns
low_findings.description: The hardcoded secrets, weak crypto, XSS, C2, malware, and reconnaissance hits are mostly examples inside security rule or reference documentation. They demonstrate vulnerable and fixed patterns rather than executing those patterns as part of the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is in documentation sections that explain vulnerability classes and remediation. I did not find evidence that these examples are executed automatically.

low_findings.title: Reference Links Are Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: Most hardcoded URL findings point to OWASP, CWE, example.com, or security documentation references. These are expected documentation links and do not show data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are cited as references or placeholders. The only network URL I found with execution impact is reported separately as the high-risk CI installer pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell in Reusable CI Asset
dangerous_patterns.description: A reusable pipeline asset uses a network download piped directly into a shell. Replace this with a pinned release, checksum verification, or a maintained GitHub Action version.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit and appears in a template intended for reuse. This is a known supply-chain risk because the downloaded script can change outside the repository.

content.value_statement: Security teams need repeatable workflows for reviews, rules, and CI security checks. This skill provides reusable templates for AppSec, DevSecOps, compliance, and incident response work.
actual_capabilities: Provides a structured SKILL.md template for security operations workflows.
Includes checklist patterns for assessment, remediation, threat modeling, compliance, and incident response.
Provides a CI security scan template covering SAST, dependency, secrets, container, and IaC checks.
Includes a rule template with OWASP, CWE, MITRE ATT&CK, and compliance mappings.
Shows vulnerable and fixed examples for common application security issues.
limitations: The skill is still a generic template and requires customization before production use.
The CI template contains an unsafe remote installer pattern that must be replaced.
The examples are educational and are not a complete security testing program.
It does not include executable scripts in the scanned file structure.

use_cases.title: Create AppSec Review Playbooks
use_cases.description: Turn common security review steps into repeatable checklists with OWASP and CWE references.

use_cases.title: Draft DevSecOps CI Templates
use_cases.description: Start from a security scanning pipeline that covers code, dependencies, secrets, containers, and IaC.

use_cases.title: Prepare Compliance Evidence Workflows
use_cases.description: Organize security control review tasks and reporting steps for SOC 2, PCI-DSS, and NIST contexts.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Security Checklist
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a checklist for reviewing authentication, authorization, input validation, secrets, and logging in my application.

prompt_templates.title: Map Findings to Standards
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the reference templates to map these findings to OWASP, CWE, MITRE ATT&CK, and compliance controls. Include severity and remediation guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Adapt a CI Security Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Customize the CI security scan template for my repository. Replace unsafe remote installers with pinned and verified installation methods.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Incident Response Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an incident response checklist for a suspected credential exposure. Include containment, evidence preservation, eradication, recovery, and follow-up tasks.

output_examples.input: Request: Create a security assessment checklist for a web application.
output: A staged checklist covering entry points, authentication, authorization, data flows, controls, findings, and reporting.
Each step includes expected evidence and a severity decision point.

output_examples.input: Request: Improve a CI security scan workflow.
output: A revised pipeline plan with SAST, dependency, secrets, container, and IaC scans.
The plan calls out pinned tools, minimal permissions, retained artifacts, and pull request feedback.

output_examples.input: Request: Map a weak hashing issue to security standards.
output: A finding summary with OWASP Cryptographic Failures and CWE weak algorithm mappings.
The remediation recommends modern hashing choices and validation steps.

best_practices: Customize placeholders before using any generated workflow or template.
Review every CI command and pin third-party tools to trusted versions.
Treat vulnerable snippets as examples only and pair them with safe remediation guidance.
anti_patterns: Do not publish the template with placeholder names, categories, or maintainer fields.
Do not pipe remote installer scripts directly into a shell in CI.
Do not treat example rules as complete coverage for a security program.

faq.question: Is this skill ready to publish as-is?
faq.answer: No. It is an unfinished template and includes an unsafe CI installer pattern.

faq.question: Does the skill contain confirmed malicious code?
faq.answer: No confirmed malicious intent was found. The main issue is risky reusable guidance.

faq.question: Why did static analysis report many severe findings?
faq.answer: Many findings come from security examples that intentionally show vulnerable code patterns.

faq.question: What must be fixed before publishing?
faq.answer: Replace the pipe-to-shell installer, customize placeholders, and review all CI permissions and commands.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What is the best use for this skill?
faq.answer: Use it as a starting point for security operations workflows, not as a complete production policy.
